Renaissance Early Modern

Glossary About
The turn inward · 3rd – 6th c. CE

Late Antiquity

Late antiquity inherits the classical question of soul and turns it toward metaphysics, theology, and spiritual transformation. Plotinus, Christian Platonists, and Byzantine thinkers such as Maximus and John of Damascus deepen the relation between intellect, embodiment, and the ascent of mind.

fl. c. 200 CE

Alexander of Aphrodisias

The great Peripatetic commentator: a rigorously naturalistic reading of Aristotle’s De Anima.

c. 204 – 270 CE

Plotinus

Soul between the One and the sensible world: the founder of Neoplatonism on interiority, intellect, and the return.

354 – 430 CE

Augustine of Hippo

The turn inward: memory, will, and the self as an interior world answerable to truth.

fl. c. 500 CE

Pseudo-Dionysius

The mystical theology that would shape a millennium of thought on what soul is and is not.

c. 477 – 524 CE

Boethius

Philosophical consolation in late antiquity: reason, providence, and the soul that still sees.

c. 675 – c. 749

John of Damascus

Christian psychology, free will, and the soul’s rational powers.

c. 580 – 662

Maximus the Confessor

Intellect, will, and the soul’s ascent through deification.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
